first_line: Last week, my manager asked me:
post_full:
Last week, my manager asked me:

Aayush, are you making "progress", or just creating the "illusion" of it?

I'd spent the last 5 years being an "execution machine." This question broke me.

See, before Atlan, I was the guy who:
- Shipped things fast
- Checked off 10+ tasks before lunch
- Worked mindlessly and felt productive

I wore my “busyness” like a badge of honor.

Then I joined Atlan. Showed my first week's progress.

My manager's response?

"You did A thing”. That's motion, not progress. 
Actual progress? What metric did it move for us? 

Wait what?

I was confused. Wasn't doing stuff... the job?

Turns out, at Atlan, every project needs:

- A problem/solution statement
- A clear metric it will move
- A process diagram

No exceptions.

My first attempt? Painful.

I sat for 3 hours trying to write a P/PS statement. 3 HOURS. 

For what used to take me 3 minutes to just start doing.

Felt like I was wasting time. Doing nothing.

Then something clicked.

Those 3 hours of thinking saved 30 hours of rework.

Now my split looks like (still WIP):
- 80% thinking
- 20% doing

The result? I do "slightly" better work in half the time.

My manager put it perfectly:

"Deep thinking takes time & willingness to question. 
First principles thinking takes time to develop. 
A powerful habit to incorporate is to make time to think."

Here's what "slowing down" taught me:

The best PMs aren't the fastest typers.

They're the deepest thinkers.

We've been trained to mistake motion for progress. 
But motion without direction is just expensive wandering.

Sometimes the most productive thing you can do is close your laptop and think.

What percentage of your day is thinking vs doing?

p.s. If your calendar is full of "doing" meetings, you might be stuck in the same trap I was :)

first_line: I quit my job with zero backup plan.
post_full:
I quit my job with zero backup plan.

Everyone said I was insane.

3 months later, I realized they were wrong about everything.

See, by January 2025, I was done:
- Mentally fried
- Living on autopilot
- Couldn't give 100% at Springworks

So I did what "responsible adults" never do.

I walked away.

- No next job lined up. 
- No 12-month runway saved.
- No side hustle to fall back on. 

Just... nothing.

My savings dropped 𝟰𝟬%. But, my anxiety dropped 𝟵𝟬%.

For the first time in my life, my calendar was empty.

And something strange happened. I became the happiest I'd ever been.

- Random coffee at 2pm? Sure. 
- Reading for 6 hours straight? Why not. 
- Deep diving into AI with no agenda? Absolutely.

Then I did something even crazier.

A, 10-day Vipassana retreat:
- No phone
- No talking
- 4:30am wake-ups
- 10 hours of meditation daily

The hardest thing I've ever done. Also the most transformative.

Here's what 3 months of "nothing" taught me:

The space between your last thing and your next thing?

That's not emptiness. 
That's where you find yourself again.

We're so obsessed with the next milestone that we forget:

Sometimes not knowing 𝗜𝗦 the plan.

If you can "afford" to take a break, do it. 

Not a vacation. Not a sabbatical with goals.

A real break. Where nothing is planned and everything is possible.

It might just be the most productive thing you never do.

p.s. Take this post as your sign to take a break from work :)

p.p.s First day at the Vipassana center 👇🏻

every startup says they're "ai-powered" now.

but 99% are just chatgpt wrappers with extra steps.

the 1% though? they're terrifying.

Don't get me WRONG, the 99% aren't dumb. 
Smart companies. Great founders. Wrong approach.

Here's why:

They're still thinking AI First when winners are going AI Native.

Most companies don't even know there's a difference.

There is. And it changes everything.

See, there are two types of companies right now:

AI First: "How can we add AI to improve X?" 
AI Native: "Why does X exist at all?"

One optimizes. One eliminates.

At Atlan, we realized going AI Native means transforming at 3 levels:

- AI Native Departments: Reimagining what Marketing, Sales, Product even do
- AI Native Talent: Every single person thinking with AI, not just using it
- AI Native Operations: Breaking down silos, AI-powered collaboration

Khosla Ventures (the $15 Bn VC) gets us:
They're buying call centers and rebuilding them AI Native.
Not improving them. Replacing their entire existence.

The uncomfortable truth:
If you're adding AI features, you're playing defense. 
If you're eliminating human constraints, you're playing offense.

Most of your processes exist because humans have limitations:
- Can't work 24/7
- Can't predict what they don't know
- Can't process 1000 data points simultaneously

Kill the constraint. Kill the process.

Ask yourself: What would you never have built if AI always existed?

Your answer might be your biggest opportunity.

Or your biggest threat if someone else figures it out first.

What would you delete if AI had always existed?

p.s. My honest answer? My entire role. That's why I'm learning to build what replaces me. 
p.p.s This is me drooling over our new N8N setup 👇🏻

first_line: I'm convinced some teenager with ChatGPT is destroying my returns.
post_full:
I'm convinced some teenager with ChatGPT is destroying my returns.

And it's keeping me up at night.

I spent 67 hours analyzing Phoenix Mills.
Bought at ₹940.
It's up 69%.

But somewhere, some kid probably typed "good stocks" and did better.

While I'm here:
- Copying whatever Akshat does
- Reading random reports at 2 AM
- Pretending I understand why it works

(I don't. I just have conviction and Excel.)

So when Zerodha dropped their Kite MCP, I did something:

Started feeding it GOLD:
- Berkshire annual letters
- Munger's mental models
- Mark Minervini's technical systems

Building an AI that thinks like I wish I could.

The uncomfortable truth?
That teenager doesn't overthink.
They just ask: "what will go up?"

While I ask: "what's the debt-to-equity ratio adjusted for seasonal fluctuations?"

Maybe that's what I'm really building -
A way to ask simple questions again.

Teaching AI to be obsessed so I don't have to be.

What weird thing are you building that no one knows about?

P.S. The AI hasn't made a single trade yet. But it did suggest I "try index funds." Even my AI thinks I overthink.

I read something nasty today 😔

And I can't stop thinking about it.

Apparently, we lost the internet to bots.

According to the 2025 Imperva Bad Bot report:
- 51% of all web traffic is now bots
- 37% of them are malicious
- Up from 32% last year

First time ever we're the minority on the Internet.

Yesterday Sam Parr posted "LinkedIn should ban AI comments".

IMO, AI comments aren't the real problem.
They're just the part we can see.

The real issue? We're optimizing for all the wrong things:
- That engagement metric you track? Mostly fake.
- That traffic spike you celebrated? Probably bots..
- That growth curve you show investors? Built on lies...

We built the internet for connection.

Handed it to the machines instead.
Now we perform for an audience that doesn't exist.

Want to know if you're talking to humans?

Check these patterns in your analytics:
- Geographic distribution that's too perfect
- Session durations that don't vary
- Traffic spikes with no real source
- Engagement at weird hours

Or don't.

The bots don't care either way.

They've already won and the jokes on us.

first_line: Last week I discovered I'm terrible
post_full:
Last week I discovered I'm terrible
at the one thing I pride myself on:

Clear communication.

The evidence was overwhelming.

I literally get paid to make 
complex AI workflows simple.

Then I wrote a "simple" team announcement.

Enter Suren.

"Nice announcement. But how many
things did you assume people know?"

I thought "Maybe 5-6 things?"

"Let's check."

We asked AI to count.
Every. Single. Assumption.

36.

Thirty-six assumptions. In one message.

Like believing people:
- Know what "n8n" means
- Will brainstorm great use cases
- See why AI enablement matters
- Understand what are AI workflows
(and more.....)

My "clear" message?
Cryptic.

Written in a language
only I understand.

Here's what hit me:

We're all doing this.
Every email. Every presentation.
Every "simple" explanation.

The curse of knowledge is real.

The more you know,
the worse you communicate.

You literally forget
what it's like to "not" know.

So here's my question:

What obvious thing do you say daily that's probably not obvious at all?

first_line: My mom asked AI to cancel her dentist appointment.
post_full:
My mom asked AI to cancel her dentist appointment.

It wrote a LinkedIn message.

147 words.
3 paragraphs.
"I am writing to formally request..."

She deleted it all.

Typed: "Can't make Saturday"

Sent.

That's when it hit me.

Every AI tool today is broken the same way:

- Gmail AI turns 5 words into 50
- Notion AI explains your notes back to you
- Calendar AI suggests meetings nobody wants
- Grammarly makes everyone sound like robots

We put rocket engines on bicycles.
Then wonder why we keep falling over.

Here's the thing:

Instead of: "AI, help me write emails"
Should be: "AI, handle all my emails"

Instead of: "AI, summarize this doc"
Should be: "AI, just show me what matters"

Instead of: "AI, improve my writing"
Should be: "AI, write like me so I don't have to"

See the difference?

Current AI = Faster way to do boring work
Real AI = Never do boring work again

Mom doesn't want a smarter assistant.

She wants her appointment canceled.
In 5 words.
Like she asked.

AI should do what we want, how we want it, without us having to manage it.

All she wanted was a simple email. AI made it complex.

But we'll get there.
We always do.

BTW - Mom asked if she can train Gmail by yelling at it like Siri. I said no. She said "Then what's the point?"

Mom: 2 | AI: 0

p.s. inspired by Pete Koomen's "AI Horseless Carriages" - a must-read on why we're building AI wrong

first_line: I asked AI one simple question about our future...
post_full:
I asked AI one simple question about our future...

The answer was terrifying

My prompt was simple:

"Based on how we operate as humans today, create an image showing humanity in 50 years if we continue this behavior"

AI had infinite futures to choose from.

Medical miracles. Space colonies. Green cities. Mundane progress. Radical transformation.

It chose dystopia.

Respirator masks. Scorched skies. Dead rivers. Digital billboards selling fake happiness.

Every sci-fi nightmare, perfectly rendered.

But here's what's surprising:

I never asked for dystopia.

AI just assumed that's what I meant.

Because that's what we've taught it "the future" means.

Through every:
- Climate headline we share
- Apocalypse movie we stream
- Doomsday prediction we click
- Black Mirror episode we binge

We've trained AI that future equals fear.

The most advanced prediction engine ever built can only imagine one tomorrow:

The one where everything breaks.
Not because that's inevitable.
Because that's all we feed it.

What does it say about us
when our machines can't dream
of anything but nightmares?

first_line: Your AI startup isn't a SaaS company.
post_full:
Your AI startup isn't a SaaS company.

It’s an all-you-can-eat buffet where your customers get twice as hungry every 6 months.

The $20/month flat-fee pricing model is a trap.

Founders are betting the farm on a simple assumption:
As compute gets cheaper, our margins will grow.

This is a fatal oversimplification.

It ignores the other side of the equation:
As AI gets more powerful, our appetite for it explodes.

Last year, a user was happy summarizing a 1-page doc.

Today, they want to analyze a 200-page PDF and ask 50 follow-up questions.

Your costs for that user just went up 100x.
But the cost of compute didn't drop 100x.
And even if it did, our ambition would just expand 1000x to meet it.

You're left holding the bag.

So what's the playbook? 

In his piece on this, Ethan Ding suggests three paths to survive:

1. Usage-Based Pricing: 
Stop selling a buffet. Charge for every plate.

2. High Switching Costs: 
Integrate deeply into a customer's workflow (especially enterprise) that leaving becomes too painful.

3. Vertical Integration: 
Make the AI a loss-leader for other, highly profitable services like hosting or databases.

The common thread is you must build a business model that profits from explosive use, instead of being drowned by it.

Which path are you choosing?

I finally broke GPT…
I asked how many r’s are in “strawberry.”

It said 2.

Meanwhile a five-year-old 
“laughing” and “counting”

S-T-R-A-W-B-E-R-R-Y. (3.)

This trillion-dollar technology that can write code, pass medical exams, and explain quantum physics... 

Can't count to 3.

Why?

LLMs are “like” your phone’s autocomplete

You type: “I’m heading to the…”
Your phone: Gym, store, office.

It does’t access your calendar, rather completes a common pattern.

LLMs do the same. 
So when you ask, “How many r’s in strawberry?” 
It’s trying to guess or predict a number

Words with three of the same letter are uncommon, so the model goes: “Probably 1… maybe 2.”

Elephant memory, goldfish math.

Meanwhile we ask AI to:
→ Analyze customer data
→ Count sales trends
→ Track inventory

…and it’s out here failing kindergarten.

So what’s the fix?
→ Make it list things first vs direct counting
→ Use code for counting. "strawberry".count("r") → 3
→ Double-check. “Do it twice independently; if different, recount.”
→ Force proof. “Line 1: the number. Line 2: the positions you used.”
 
Quick test:

“How many p’s in hippopotamus?”
Correct: 3.
ChatGPT (too often): “Definitely 2.”

This is honestly the funniest thing I’ve learned all year.

Here’s a small explainer on this topic ↓
